title-group.article-title: Upregulation of GSTP1 mediated by chimeric TFE3 promotes TFE3-tRCC progression by targeting JNK signaling pathway

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/026axqv54grid.428392.60000 0004 1800 1685Department of Urology, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital Clinical College of Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, Jiangsu Nanjing, 210008 China

3https://ror.org/01rxvg760grid.41156.370000 0001 2314 964XDepartment of Urology, Medical School, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210002 China
4https://ror.org/026axqv54grid.428392.60000 0004 1800 1685Department of Urology, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital Clinical College of Southeast University, Jiangsu Nanjing, 210008 China

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: TFE3-translocation renal cell carcinoma (TFE3-tRCC), a distinct subtype of kidney cancer characterized by Xp11.2 translocations, involving TFE3 fusion with various partner genes, lacks effective treatments and prognostic biomarkers for advanced stages. This study aimed to unravel the pathogenic mechanisms and uncover novel therapeutic targets.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The transcriptional characterization of TFE3-tRCC was conducted by RNA sequencing on 14 untreated primary TFE3-tRCC patients. The relative mRNA and protein levels were detected using qRT-PCR and Western blot, respectively. The location of ASPL-TFE3 fusion protein was analyzed by immunofluorescence. MTT and colony formation assays were used to detect cell proliferation. Annexin V/PI staining was used to evaluate cell apoptosis. Transwell assays were used to evaluate in vitro cell migration and invasion.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In TFE3-tRCC patients, GSTP1 expression was upregulated. ASPL-TFE3 cell models revealed that the ASPL-TFE3 fusion protein translocates to the nucleus, contributing to tumorigenesis. Notably, GSTP1 was transcriptionally activated by chimeric TFE3. Treatment with GSTP1-targeting siRNA or the GSTP1 inhibitor Ezatiostat effectively inhibited tumor growth and induced apoptosis in TFE3-tRCC cells. Furthermore, GSTP1 was found to drive TFE3-tRCC progression via modulation of the JNK signaling pathway.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Upregulation of GSTP1 mediated by chimeric TFE3 promotes TFE3-tRCC progression by targeting JNK signaling pathway, which underscore the potential of GSTP1 as a promising therapeutic target for TFE3-tRCC.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12957-024-03633-w.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2024

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is common cancer with annually more than four hundred thousand new cases in the world [1]. TFE3-translocation RCC (TFE3-tRCC) is a rare but aggressive form of RCC. It is defined by the WHO as an independent subtype of RCC and classified into microphthalmia transcription factor/transcription factor E (MiT/TFE) family tRCC [2, 3]. This type of cancer is characterized by the breakage and fusion of the TFE3 gene located in the short arm of the X chromosome, which results in TFE3 translocation to partner genes with strong promoters and produces a highly active chimeric TFE3 protein [4]. The chimeric TFE3 protein is localized in the nucleus, whereas the TFE3 protein is primarily localized in the cytoplasm [5, 6].

TFE3 is a proto-oncogene, which is associated with nutrient sensing, lysosomal biogenesis, and autophagy [7]. The overexpression of the TFE3 fusion gene leads to tumorigenesis and tumor progression [8, 9]. This is particularly evident in patients under the age of 45 years, with a very poor prognosis [5]. The fusion of TFE3 with other genes is believed to amplify TFE3's intrinsic pro-cancer properties, leading to the upregulation of oncogenes due to the introduction of a novel and more potent promoter. More than 10 fusion subtypes have been reported, including ASPL-TFE3, PRCC-TFE3, NONO-TFE3, RBM10-TFE3, SFPQ1-TFE3, and others [10–13]. The most common subtype is ASPL-TFE3 tRCC caused by t (X; 17) (p11; q25) translocation, and this subtype has a poorer prognosis compared to other subtypes of tRCC [6]. The breakpoint sites in TFE3 fusion are different even for the same partner, resulting in various isomers. However, exons 6–10 of TFE3, which include the basic helix-loop-helix-leucine zipper (bHLH-LZ) and transcriptional activation domain, are always retained and can recognize the genome's transcription initiation or E-box sites [5, 9]. Strong evidence supports TFE3 fusion as an oncogenic driver with constitutive transcriptional activity [14]. The previous gene expression profile data suggested that TFE3-tRCC may be inconsistent with immunotherapies targeting conventional clear cell renal cell carcinoma. Multikinase inhibitors and immunotherapies using cytokines have shown limited response in patients with metastatic TFE3-tRCC. The molecular basis of TFE3 gene fusion in tRCC remains unelucidated, and effective targeted therapies have yet to be determined. Therefore, further understanding the pathogenic mechanism of TFE3-tRCC will be helpful to identify novel potentially therapeutic targets.

In this study, we applied RNA-seq on primary TFE3-tRCCs to investigate their transcriptional characterization, and focused on a hubgene, GSTP1, to explore the mechanism and potential therapeutic target in TFE3-tRCC. We found that GSTP1 promoted the progression of TFE3-tRCC by targeting JNK signaling pathway and GSTP1 may potentially be a therapeutic target in TFE3-tRCC.

sec.title: Patients and tissue samples
sec.p: Tissue samples from a total of 33 tRCC patients who underwent surgical resection in Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital were included in this study. Initially, 14 collected between 2015 and 2020 were used for RNA-seq, and 19 others were used for immunohistochemistry (IHC). All patients signed the informed consent forms. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital.

sec.title: Cell culture
sec.p: Two cell lines 786–O and ACHN, derived from human clear cell RCC and human renal cell adenocarcinoma respectively, were cultured in DMEM or RPMI 1640 (Multicell) supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin–streptomycin in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

sec.title: Lentivirus and siRNA constructs
sec.p: ASPL-TFE3 fusion coding sequence was synthesized based on the ASPL's 1–7 exons and TFE3's 6–10 exons and inserted in a lentiviral vector GenePharma (Shanghai, China). siRNAs were designed and synthesized by Generay biotech (Shanghai, China). The siRNA sequences are provided in Supplementary Table S1.

sec.title: RNA isolation and quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) assays
sec.p:
Total RNA from tRCC tissues or cultured cells was isolated using Trizol reagent. The RNA was reversely transcribed to cDNA with Hiscript Q RT Super-mix for qPCR (Vazyme, Nanjing, China), followed by quantification with real-time PCR with ChamQ SYBR qPCR Master Mix (Vazyme, Nanjing, China), and the data were acquired using the Applied Biosystems StepOne Real-Time PCR System (Applied Biosystems, CA, USA). β-actin was chosen as an internal control for normalization. The specific primers used were as follows:
ASPL-TFE3
5’-CCCCCTTTGTTCCTTTCT-3’ (forward) and
5’-GCCCGACTTGGACTTCTT-3’(reverse).
GSTP1
5’-TTGGGCTCTATGGGAAGGAC-3’ (forward) and
5’-GGGAGATGTATTTGCAGCGGA-3’(reverse).
β-actin
5’-CATGTACGTTGCTATCCAGGC-3’ (forward) and.
5’-CTCCTTAATGTCACGCACGAT-3’(reverse).

sec.title: IHC
sec.p: The paraffin-embedded sections were deparaffinized and incubated with rabbit polyclonal anti-GSTP1 (1:800) (ABclonal, Wuhan, China) at 4 °C overnight. After three times wash with PBST, the sections were added with HRP conjugated goat anti-rabbit secondary antibody (1:1000) at room temperature 1 h. Followed three washes, the resultant signal was detected using a DAB Substrate Kit (ZSGB-BIO, Beijing, China).

sec.title: Immunofluorescence
sec.p: The cells were incubated sequentially with primary and secondary antibodies overnight. Glass bottom was mounted with DAPI and fluorescent images were captured using a confocal microscope (EVOS™ Auto 2).

sec.title: MTT and colony-forming assays
sec.p: Transfected cells were seeded in the plates and a cell proliferation assay was performed using 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Transfected cells were seeded into a 12-well plate with 200 cells per well and assessed for their colony-forming capacity after 7–14 days.

sec.title: Cell migration and invasion assay
sec.p: To measure cell migration ability, we conducted a Transwell cell migration assay using an 8-μm pore size filter. We seeded 5 × 104 cells into the upper chambers and allowed them to migrate for 4 h. After that, we fixed and stained the migrated cells using 4% paraformaldehyde and 1% crystal violet. In the cell invasion experiment, we pre-coated the upper chambers with Matrigel and then followed the same protocol used in the cell migration assay.

sec.title: Flow cytometry
sec.p: Flow cytometry was carried out as per the manufacturer's instructions. Following incubation with reagents from an Annexin V-647/Propidium Iodide (PI) Apoptosis Kit (Yeasen Biotechnology), cells were analyzed with NovoCyte and FlowJo software. For cell cycle analysis, cells were incubated with reagents from a PI/RNase Staining Kit (Beyotime) and then analyzed with NovoCyte.

sec.title: Animal experiment
sec.p: Male nude mice at the age of five to six weeks were provided by Gempharmatech (Nanjing, China). ACHN cells were transfected with LV-ASPL-TFE3 and either shRNA (GSTP1) or shRNA (ctrl). 5 × 106 cells were injected subcutaneously and maintained in specific pathogen-free conditions. After 60 days, the tumors were resected for analyzing weight and volume. The animal experiments were conducted based on the guidelines of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical analyses were performed using IBM SPSS Statistics 24 and GraphPad Prism 9. Student’s t-test and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) were used to assess the significance of differences. A p-value of less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. All values are expressed as means ± standard deviation.

sec.title: The transcriptional characterization of TFE3-tRCC
sec.p: In this study, we conducted a comprehensive transcriptional profiling of 14 tumor tissues and 3 adjacent non-neoplastic tissues from patients diagnosed with TFE3-tRCC (Supplementary Table S2). A differential gene threshold of |log2 FG|> 1 and an adjusted

p-value < 0.05 were used for the analysis. We identified a total of 931 genes exhibiting significant differential expression, with 672 genes upregulated and 259 genes downregulated. These findings are visually represented in the volcano plot (Fig. 1A) and the heatmap of the top 50 differentially expressed genes (Fig. 1B), highlighting their up- or downregulation patterns. To gain insights into the functional implications of these changes, we performed Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment analyses. The GO biological process analysis revealed organic anion transport as the most significantly enriched pathway (Fig. 1C), suggesting a potential role in TFE3-tRCC. Furthermore, KEGG enrichment analysis indicated significant upregulation of pathways related to lysosome and glutathione metabolism in TFE3-tRCC (Fig. 1D and E), emphasizing the metabolic alterations associated with this malignancy. In addition, Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) uncovered enrichment of transcriptional pathways implicated in proliferation (e.g., MYC targets, E2F targets, and G2M checkpoint), oxidative phosphorylation, mTOR signaling, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) signaling (Supplementary Fig. S1A and B). Finally, we employed CIBERSORT to characterize the immune cell landscape in TFE3-tRCC samples. Our analysis revealed that M2 macrophages and resting memory CD4+ T cells constituted the predominant infiltrating immune cell populations (Fig. 1F).Fig. 1
Transcriptomic analysis of differentially expressed genes by RNA-seq in tissues of TFE3-tRCC. A Volcano plot of differentially expressed genes. B Heatmap of top 50 up-regulated/down-regulated significant genes. C Gene Ontology enrichment analysis of differentially expressed genes. D, E Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes enrichment analysis of differentially expressed genes. F The abundance of immune cell populations in TFE3-tRCC samples

sec.title: TFE3-tRCCs display upregulation of GSTP1 gene
sec.p:
To elucidate the pivotal genes involved in the pathogenesis of TFE3-tRCC, we constructed a protein–protein interaction (PPI) network for differentially expressed genes leveraging the STRING database (Supplementary Fig. S1C). Subsequently, we identified the hub genes utilizing the cytoHub plugin within the Cytoscape software platform. Among these, GSR, GPX2, GSS, GSTP1, and GSTM3 emerged as the top five hub genes (Fig. 2A). To validate their expression patterns, we mined data from the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) database, focusing on tumor samples harboring TFE3 fusions. As depicted in Fig. 2B, a marked upregulation of GSTP1 and GSS was observed in TFE3-tRCC, whereas GSTM3 expression was notably downregulated. These findings concurred with our RNA-sequencing data.Fig. 2
GSTP1 is upregulated in TFE3-tRCC. A The hub genes identified as Top5 were GSR, GPX2, GSS, GSTP1, and GSTM3. B The relative expression of Hub genes between tRCC tissues and adjacent tissues in TCGA database. C, E Correlation analysis of GSTP1, GSS or GSTM3 with TFE3. F, G GSTP1 expression was detected in TFE3-tRCC tumor tissues and adjacent tissues in the TCGA database and Wang et al.’s data. H, I GSTP1 expression was detected in KIRC or KIRP tumor tissues and corresponding adjacent tissues in the TCGA database. J Representative images of IHC staining (GSTP1) of the tumor. Scale bar = 100 μ. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001

To delve deeper, we examined the correlation between these statistically significant genes and TFE3 expression. Notably, a robust positive correlation was uncovered between GSTP1 and TFE3 expression (Fig. 2C-E). Both the KEGG enrichment analysis and hub gene identification pointed to glutathione metabolism as a crucial pathway in TFE3-tRCC. Given GSTP1's pivotal role in glutathione metabolism and its close association with tumorigenesis and progression, research on GSTP1 in the context of cancer has garnered considerable attention.

To corroborate our findings, we analyzed GSTP1's differential expression using the TCGA database and data from Wang et al. [15], which consistently revealed a significant upregulation of GSTP1 in TFE3-tRCC (Fig. 2F and G). Additionally, Sun et al.'s data also demonstrated GSTP1 overexpression in TFE3-tRCC samples [16]. However, contrasting results emerged from the TCGA database for KIRC and KIRP, with GSTP1 downregulated in KIRC but no significant difference observed in KIRP (Fig. 2H and I). To further validate our findings, we conducted IHC analysis, which once again confirmed the significant upregulation of GSTP1 in TFE3-tRCC specimens (Fig. 2J). To sum up, these data suggest the distinctive upregulation of GSTP1 in TFE3-tRCC compared to other types of kidney cancer and underscore the potential role of GSTP1 as a key player in TFE3-tRCC pathogenesis.

sec.title: The ASPL-TFE3 protein plays a crucial role in maintaining cell viability
sec.p:
The prevailing "dysregulated activity model" currently posits that fusion proteins augment the intrinsic oncogenic potential of wild-type TFE3 proteins by introducing more active transcriptional promoters [8]. To investigate the role and mechanism of GSTP1 in TFE3-tRCC, we created an artificial chimeric gene by joining ASPL exon 7 to TFE3 exon 6 (Fig. 3A), following a previously established protocol [6]. After infecting the 786-O and ACHN cell lines with lentiviral vectors harboring the ASPL-TFE3 construct, qRT-PCR analysis using ASPL-TFE3-specific primers revealed significant upregulation of ASPL-TFE3 mRNA levels (Fig. 3B). Western blot and immunofluorescence staining revealed the predominantly nuclear localization and significant upregulation of ASPL-TFE3 fusion protein (Fig. 3C and D). Thus, this system stably overexpressed ASPL-TFE3 fusion protein in the nucleus.Fig. 3
The TFE3 fusion protein accumulated in the nucleus and promoted the progression of TFE3-tRCC. A An artificial chimeric gene by joining ASPL exon 7 to TFE3 exon 6. B, C The relative mRNA and protein expression of ASPL-TFE3 in 786-O and ACHN transfected with lentivirus. D Immunofluorescence of TFE3 in the nuclear fractions of RCC cells overexpressing ASPL-TFE3. E MTT assay of 786-O and ACHN cells with ASPL-TFE3 overexpression. F Colony-forming assay of the proliferation ability of 786-O and ACHN cells with or without ASPL-TFE3 overexpression. G Apoptosis cells were assessed by flow cytometry assay in the cells with or without ASPL-TFE3 overexpression. H Transwell assay in 786-O and ACHN cells treated with or without ASPL-TFE3 overexpression. *P < 0.05,**P < 0.01,***P < 0.001,****P < 0.0001

To further elucidate the functional implications of the ASPL-TFE3 fusion protein, we conducted MTT and cell colony-forming assays in lentiviral-infected cells, both with and without the ASPL-TFE3 fusion. Our findings indicated that, compared to the negative control, the presence of ASPL-TFE3 fusion protein significantly augmented the proliferative capacity of 786-O and ACHN cells (Fig. 3E and F). Flow cytometry analysis further corroborated this, revealing a marked decrease in the apoptosis rate of 786-O and ACHN cells upon ASPL-TFE3 overexpression (Fig. 3G). Moreover, Transwell assays demonstrated that the ASPL-TFE3 fusion protein significantly potentiated the migratory and invasive abilities of these cells (Fig. 3H). In summary, these results consolidate the role of ASPL-TFE3 fusion protein as an active oncogenic factor, expediting the progression of tRCC.

sec.title: Chimeric TFE3 transcriptionally activates GSTP1
sec.p:
In our study, qRT-PCR and Western blot analyses revealed a pronounced upregulation of GSTP1 in response to ASPL-TFE3 overexpression, compared to the negative control group (Fig. 4A and B). This observation hints at a potential regulatory mechanism wherein ASPL-TFE3 overexpression mediates the upregulation of GSTP1. Prior investigations have established that chimeric TFE3 proteins typically retain their basic helix-loop-helix leucine zipper (bHLH-LZ) domain, which is critical for DNA binding, and the GSTP1 promoter harbors a CACGTC E-box motif known to interact with such domains [5]. This alignment suggests that chimeric TFE3 may transcriptionally regulate GSTP1, given that CACGTG E-box elements are known to bind the bHLH-LZ structure [17]. To further substantiate this hypothesis, we employed the JASPAR database (https://jaspar.genereg.net/) to predict the three most enriched motifs within regions that are likely to serve as TFE3 binding sites (Fig. 4C). Subsequently, we utilized ChIP-qPCR to validate the direct interaction between chimeric TFE3 and the GSTP1 promoter region, specifically targeting those segments containing the predicted motifs. Our results conclusively demonstrated the direct binding of chimeric TFE3 to the GSTP1 promoter, thereby confirming its role in transcriptionally regulating GSTP1 (Fig. 4D and E).Fig. 4
Chimeric TFE3 transcriptionally upregulates GSTP1. A, B The relative mRNA and protein expression of GSTP1 treated with ASPL-TFE3 overexpression. C The three most enriched sequence motifs between chimeric TFE3 and GSTP1 promoter identified by JASPAR. D Scheme indicating the primer sets used for ChIP-qPCR. E ChIP-qPCR was used to detect the binding of TFE3 to the GSTP1 promoter. *P < 0.05,**P < 0.01,***P < 0.001,****P < 0.0001

sec.title: GSTP1 promotes the progression of TFE3-tRCC
sec.p:
To delve into the biological significance of GSTP1 in the development of TFE3-tRCC, we conducted a series of experiments involving the specific knockdown of GSTP1 using GSTP1-targeted siRNA (siGSTP1), alongside a non-specific siRNA as a negative control (siNC), in ASPL-TFE3-overexpressing 786-O and ACHN cells (Fig. 5A). Our MTT assay revealed that the proliferation of these cells was significantly attenuated upon GSTP1 knockdown, as compared to cells transfected with siNC (Fig. 5B). Furthermore, colony-forming assays demonstrated a notable reduction in the colony-forming ability of ASPL-TFE3-overexpressing cells following GSTP1 inhibition (Fig. 5C). Flow cytometry analysis confirmed an increase in apoptosis among GSTP1-knockdown cells, indicating a pivotal role of GSTP1 in modulating cell survival (Fig. 5D). When an inhibitor of GSTP1, Ezatiostat, was added to treat 786-O and ACHN cells with overexpressed ASPL-TFE3 at half maximal inhibitory concentration (Supplementary Fig. S2A), the cell apoptosis was significantly increased in the flow cytometry analysis (Supplementary Fig. S2B). To extend our understanding of the role of GSTP1 in the in vivo progression of tRCC, we generated stable ASPL-TFE3 overexpressing ACHN cell lines with or without GSTP1 knockdown (Fig. 5E) and employed this model in nude mouse experiments. Our findings demonstrated a marked suppression of tRCC tumor growth upon GSTP1 knockdown, as evidenced by the results presented in Fig. 5F. These data not only show the significance of GSTP1 in tRCC progression but also suggest its potential as a therapeutic target for the treatment of this malignancy.Fig. 5
Suppression of tRCC aggressiveness by GSTP1 knockdown in vitro and in vivo. A The relative protein expression of GSTP1 in 786-O and ACHN cells overexpressing ASPL-TFE3 with or without GSTP1 siRNA. B MTT assay of ASPL-TFE3-overexpressed cells with GSTP1 knockdown by siRNA. C Colony-forming assay of the proliferation ability of ASPL-TFE3-overexpressed cells with or without GSTP1 knockdown. D Apoptosis cells with or without GSTP1 siRNA were assessed by flow cytometry assay. E The relative protein expression of GSTP1 in ACHN cells overexpressing ASPL-TFE3 with or without GSTP1 shRNA. F A total of 5 × 106 ACHN cells suspended in PBS were injected subcutaneously into the backs of 8 mice per group. The total weight of representative murine lesions were measured at day 60. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01,***P < 0.001,****P < 0.0001

sec.title: GSTP1 regulates JNK signaling pathway
sec.p:
A non-enzymatic reaction can occur between GSTP1 and a kinase, forming a complex through protein–protein interactions that can sequester the kinase and act as a negative regulator of this stress kinase to inhibit downstream signal transmission of the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway [18], which plays a crucial role in cellular survival and death signaling [19]. The most notable characteristic of GSTP1 as a chaperone is that GSTP1 can sequester the c-jun N-terminal kinase (JNK) that is an evolutionarily conserved sub-group of MAPKs [20, 21]. While the regulatory relationship between GSTP1 and JNK signaling has been previously established in some cancers, the interaction in TFE3-tRCC remained unexplored. In our study, we observed a significant increase in JNK phosphorylation upon GSTP1 knockdown and a corresponding decrease upon GSTP1 overexpression (Fig. 6A and B). The immunoprecipitation assay further confirmed the direct interaction between GSTP1 and JNK (Fig. 6C). The above results revealed the mechanism of GSTP1 on regulating JNK signaling pathway in TFE3-tRCC. All our findings suggest that GSTP1 promotes the progression of TFE3-tRCC by targeting JNK signaling pathway (Fig. 6D).Fig. 6
GSTP1 regulates JNK signaling pathway. A, B The relatvie protein expression of JNK, p-JNK and GSTP1 transfected with GSTP1 siRNA and GSTP1 overexpressing plasmid in 786-O and ACHN cells overexpressing ASPL-TFE3. C Lysates from cells were immunoprecipitated with anti-GSTP1 antibody and then, immunoblotted with JNK antibody. D Proposed mechanisms for the promotion of GSTP1 on TFE3-tRCC development

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In our study, we delineated the transcriptional landscape of TFE3-tRCC, highlighting the pivotal role of not only the lysosomal pathway but also the glutathione metabolism as a salient feature of this malignancy. Furthermore, our study revealed that the TFE3 fusion protein transcriptionally activates GSTP1, a key player in glutathione metabolism. Notably, GSTP1 exerts its regulatory influence by modulating JNK phosphorylation. These findings collectively suggest that the chimeric TFE3-mediated upregulation of GSTP1 potentially promotes the progression of TFE3-tRCC by impeding JNK signaling pathway, thereby providing novel insights into the molecular mechanism underpinning this aggressive tumor type.

Since the discovery of TFE3 fusion proteins, the substitution of the TFE3 promoter has been considered a key molecular event in TFE3-tRCC, leading to abnormal TFE3 protein activity. ASPL is the most common of the known TFE3 gene fusion partners [11, 15, 22]. It is reported that ASPL-TFE3 has a relatively fixed structure; that is, exon 7 of ASPL and exon 6 of TFE3 are combined [16]. Here, we generated a cell system that stably overexpressed ASPL-TFE3. Results from the evaluation of our ASPL-TFE3-overexpressing cell models demonstrate that ASPL-TFE3 fusion proteins show remarkable nuclear retention and promote cell viability and tumor metastasis. The results support that chimeric TFE3 is an oncogene and promotes the progression of TFE3-tRCC.

Aberrant upregulation of TFE3 fusion-mediated transcriptional target genes, subsequently perturbing the transcriptional regulatory network, is implicated as a pivotal event in the pathogenesis of TFE3-tRCC [8]. In this study, we observed that ASPL-TFE3 overexpression led to the activation of GSTP1. Inspection of the GSTP1 promoter region revealed the presence of E-box sequences, which serve as binding sites for the bHLH-LZ transcription factors, particularly the CACGTG E-box [17, 23, 24]. Notably, all fusion genes reported in TFE3-tRCC encode the carboxy-terminal portion of TFE3, encompassing the intact bHLH-LZ domain [8, 22]. Based on these findings, we hypothesized that these fusion proteins may function as oncogenic transcription factors by binding to the E-box within the GSTP1 promoter region. To validate this hypothesis, we conducted ChIP followed by qPCR, which confirmed our prediction. This result is also in line with previous observations demonstrating the ability of the bHLH-LZ structure to bind to all CACGTG E-box motifs [17].

GSTP1 exhibits heightened expression in a diverse array of cancer types and their precancerous counterparts, underscoring its clinical significance as a dual entity—serving both as a tumor marker and a factor conferring resistance against certain anticancer therapies [18]. Our investigation specifically revealed an upregulation of GSTP1 in TFE3-tRCC compared to KIRC and KIRP, suggesting the distinctive transcriptional features in TFE3-tRCC. Furthermore, we observed significant enrichment of the glutathione metabolism pathway, in which GSTP1 plays a pivotal role, in TFE3-tRCC. This finding may provide critical insights into the robust drug resistance and unfavorable prognosis observed in this cancer subtype.

Elevated expression of GSTP1 is a pivotal factor in carcinogenesis and tumor growth, as evidenced by numerous in vitro and in vivo studies [25]. Across various tumor types characterized by high GSTP1 levels, patients frequently exhibit more aggressive tumor phenotypes, poorer prognosis, and increased resistance to chemotherapy drugs [26]. Ezatiostat is, to date, the most specific GSTP inhibitor, exhibiting a binding affinity surpassing that of GSH itself and demonstrating over 50-fold greater selectivity for GSTP compared to the GSTM and GSTA classes [27]. Currently, Ezatiostat is being investigated in preclinical and clinical studies for the treatment of myelodysplastic syndromes. In our study, functional experiments utilizing siRNA-mediated knockdown of GSTP1 and treatment with Ezatiostat demonstrated a marked increase in cell apoptosis and inhibition of cell proliferation. Furthermore, animal models revealed that GSTP1 knockdown significantly decelerated the growth rate of TFE3-tRCC. These findings underscore the promoting role of high GSTP1 expression in the progression of TFE3-tRCC.

The non-enzymatic functions of GSTP1 are increasingly recognized for their pivotal roles in biological processes [28]. Recent findings indicate that GSTP1 engages in intricate interactions with various regulatory kinases, thereby modulating signaling cascades central to cellular proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis [18, 29–33]. Additionally, GSTP1 serves as a pivotal mediator in the S-glutathionylation of numerous proteins, particularly under conditions of oxidative stress, both in vivo and in vitro. Emerging evidence highlights GSTP1's independent role in ferroptosis defense, distinct from GPX4 [34]. Our study employed Western blot and immunoprecipitation assays to elucidate the mechanism underlying GSTP1's regulation of JNK phosphorylation. We observed that GSTP1 forms a complex with JNK, modulating its phosphorylation status. This finding suggests that cancer cells with GSTP1 overexpression in TFE3-tRCC may exhibit heightened intrinsic JNK inhibitory activity. Our results support the hypothesis that upregulation of GSTP1 mediated by chimeric TFE3 promotes TFE3-tRCC progression by targeting the JNK signaling pathway.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In summary, our findings reveal a specific upregulation of GSTP1 in TFE3-tRCC tissues, which is mediated by the chimeric TFE3 protein. Our study shows the potential therapeutic implications of targeting the GSTP1 signaling pathway in TFE3-tRCC, as overexpression of GSTP1 promotes tumor progression via inhibition of the JNK signaling pathway. Thus, strategies aimed at modulating GSTP1 activity may represent a promising avenue for the treatment of TFE3-tRCC.

def.p: Transcription factor binding to IGHM enhancer 3
Alveolar soft part sarcoma chromosome region, candidate 1

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: Conceptualization: Gutian Zhang; Methodology: Weixu Chen, Mengtong Wu and Lin Du; Software: Changhua Fang, Hao Wang and Wendi Wang; Validation: Weixu Chen; Formal Analysis: Weixu Chen; Investigation: Weixu Chen; Resources: Gutian Zhang, Hongqian Guo and Chengwei Zhang; Writing – Original Draft Preparation: Weixu Chen; Writing – Review & Editing: Gutian Zhang; Supervision: Gutian Zhang, Hongqian Guo and Chengwei Zhang; Project Administration: Gutian Zhang. All authors read and approved the submitted version.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.12174192, No. 81902581), Basic Research Program (Natural Science Foundation) of Jiangsu Province (BK20190117) and China Postdoctoral Foundation (2019M660111).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital (2023–320). Informed consent was obtained from all patients. The animal study was approved by the Ethics Committee of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital. The study was conducted in accordance with the local legislation and institutional requirements.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors have no conflict of interest.
